speaker: interviewer
text: Shmuel, you're back in Poppendorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Completely confined to this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoners, of sorts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded you? Who kept you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: English military personnel.

speaker: interviewer
text: English soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you say you were there for two months. What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were taken to a city in Germany [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] called Emden. It's a well-known city in Germany, on the-- what sea? In the Baltic Sea, I think it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: The British took you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were-- we were in a camp again, but not enclosed, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could come and go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go wherever I want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except Israel. Or except Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except Palestine, except Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you remain in Emden?

speaker: interviewee
text: One short story about Emden. You told me before, if I have a story [INAUDIBLE]. I started to do a little bit of business in Emden.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought gold, people that had to sell bits and pieces of jewelry. And I got a contact with an SS man-- an ex-SS man-- who got-- he showed me the distinction of-- don't remember, high distinction. SS man.

speaker: interviewer
text: A military medal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, military medal. And he sold me, from time to time, different things. He knew-- he knew, of course, that I'm Jewish. And he-- I knew his story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know he was an SS man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was upfront about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Upfront. He had already one hand. It was after the war. For being an SS man, but he didn't get arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told you that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] a lot of people escaped. So, yes, he told me. But what I come to is an interesting story. One day, he told me he has got a tiara. You know what a tiara is? This is a diamond, like, a crown that sits like this on the hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: A tiara.

speaker: interviewee
text: A tiara.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be today worth $1 million, an enormous amount. Maybe many, much more. But many stones, diamonds. Yeah, a full crown. I said, look, I don't have any connection where to sell it. I have got a relative, a far relative in Stuttgart. I'll have to take it there and show it to him. Mr. Huber, take it there. It's like, you're not afraid I will take it away? I can take it, and you will never see it again. [GERMAN] 100%. I could have taken it away. [INAUDIBLE] Didn't then, because he wanted too much. But anyway, I went to Stuttgart. In short, somebody said the money, he said it's too expensive. I brought it back. He was sure I would not take it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he would have killed you before, but he trusted you 100% after?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way it was presented to the Germans was that we are some kinds of animals, gangsters, robbers. He trusted me this much. Which means, either the-- the propaganda cannot succeed fully, or, after a while, he saw that I'm not inhuman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never did-- you never did business with the tiara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with the tiara, but otherwise yes. The tiara I gave him back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained in Emden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. But let's say after three-quarters of a year, '48, '47, sometime in '48 we were taken to a place called Bergen-Belsen. This, you have heard, perhaps. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Bergen-Belsen, at the beginning of '48, was what sort of a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not beginning. Was not the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was later? More into '48?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hot or cold weather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cold weather, what I would say. Cold weather. In Bergen-Belsen, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] people were doing business, you know, Jewish people, back to their ways. There was lots of--

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- Bergen-Belsen at this point, was a big enormous--

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: --displaced persons camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. By the way, in Bergen-Belsen, I still worked by digging up graves and sorting out again. Because a lot of Jewish people died in Bergen-Belsen in the last minute before the-- not in the last minute-- the last day-- days [INAUDIBLE], before they were freed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were working with these corpses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Corpses, yes. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Now let me think. Another thing I remember in Bergen-Belsen, I used to come to the library, there. Quite often there was a Jewish lady. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She knew me already by name. And she said, Mr. Huber. I don't like it. You're reading too much. And I said, what do you like-- what don't you-- why don't you like to read so much? If you have time so much to read, you have got no time to earn money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Told me off-- I read too much. Tidbits. Tidbits.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you digging up the graves there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you digging up the graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. A few weeks. Wait a minute, because you cannot talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the work I did. Otherwise, I didn't do any work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the establishment of the state of Israel in Bergen-Belsen. I can remember the day when, in the radio, I heard that the United Nations, they were counting. You know the resolution? I heard this in Bergen-Belsen. So this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the whole camp was listening to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. From there, I went to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israel from Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop for a second. The counting that you heard, the United Nations vote.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your emotions-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: --very emotional. I was standing with my future wife. We were standing in the main square. Counting-- you know. It was as it goes. And we were just standing like, this was such a historical event. And this is stuck in our mind, of course. This is stuck. The counting. The counting is stuck in our minds.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could hear the delegates saying, yes, no, abstain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction within the camp once the vote had been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I wanted to tell you, unfortunately, some people take interest in everything. And some people do not think-- take interesting. Nothing. I wasn't only-- there were quite a number of people. But I cannot say everybody was [INAUDIBLE]. People were standing, because it's such a historical event.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once the gates to-- to, now, Israel were open-- it was no longer Palestine--

speaker: interviewee
text: No longer Palestine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Israel [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in May, '49. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another camp. Another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this camp? Which part of Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's not far from Haifa. About-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about an hour's walk from Haifa. And why do I remember how long it was from Haifa? There was very monotonous food, you know. And I decided I would go to Haifa and buy a bottle of kerosene. You know, bring it back, so we can cook, because everything was just so regimental. I went to Haifa and bought the bottle-- bottle back. On the way, on the [INAUDIBLE] back, the bottle slipped out and broke. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the end--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two hours. That was the end of this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the great feast.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, by the way, at this time, while I was in a camp, you couldn't go out. It was surrounded. You couldn't go out. You were not permitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Haifa. Not Haifa. The camp was outside Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Israelis were standing outside. They couldn't go out. But what was my luck? By that time I was already speaking Hebrew. So when I approached the gate, I talked to him in Hebrew. He didn't even think that I'm just arrived from overseas. Because he-- his Hebrew wasn't good enough yet. He was just a new policeman. And I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd picked up enough to convince him that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had a good background, of course, in Hebrew from my old days in Falenica. But so I just moved in and out just with a lot of chutzpah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you not allowed to move out of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you why. Because it's a time, there was no room for people where to go. And it was a matter of organizing where to send people. And [INAUDIBLE] just going in and out, they just wanted to have a system, a certain system.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they needed to get things organized and sorted out.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was hundreds of thousands coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you managed to bypass this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bypass, just because I, with my knowledge of the language, they just didn't dare imagine that I just arrived two days ago, four, five days ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: There? Just nothing. We just stayed and wait. After a while, I approach them and said, I would like to look for a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] flat on my own. My own. Because a certain amount of money I had on my own. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I found-- my in-laws found-- I want for them a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I married the 31st of March-- March, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were your in-laws to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-laws to be, yes. They knew already. And I found to them a space that I don't think an Australian would let in a dog. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Haifa. In the Arab Quarter somewhere. It was-- and afterwards, I found a place for me in along-- in a building opposite-- opposite where the boats came in in Haifa. It was an office building. But somehow I got one room. And this is where-- this is where I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you married in 1950?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What work do I do? The first year I worked as a painter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next time, the next few years, I worked as a government employee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you were part of the Israeli bureaucracy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part-- I was part of the Israeli bureaucracy. It sounds a bit in a derogative term, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. I'll take it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say-- '49, '57-- exactly eight years. May I left. May-- May '57 I left Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: By this time, did you and Rachel have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was already two years old. No, she was two years old in the boat in sail on-- on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a toddler, a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: A toddler, two years old, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to leave Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1956, there was the first war. What was this war called with the Arabs. This was the Suez war. And I just-- I don't know, can you call it a nervous breakdown? It is usually very hard to-- to say what is a nervous breakdown. You don't see any pieces. But I felt, just, after all these years, I can't anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd had enough of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just can't anymore. See? And my mother-in-law had her brother here from years ago, that lived here. And, a sister, she had here. So they organized for us papers. And I decided I'd come here, and live a quiet life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came with your in-laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Rachel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And little daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Little girl. And since you have been in Australia, what have you done?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have worked for a while as a traveler for a luggage company, manufacturing luggage. Manufacturing. At that time, it wasn't imported like now. In Marrickville. Sydenham, have you heard of Sydenham? A place not far from Marrickville.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to get up in the morning. I lived in Dover Heights here. And go by-- by bus to Central Railway. And Central Railway to Sydenham, in a bus to the factory. And I earned 15 pounds a week. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But after months and a half, they decided I should be their traveler. You know? Not work in the factory but-- Do the selling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do the selling. And I was quite successful. Got improvement in payment from 15 to 25 pounds. At that time, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Big difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: Big difference. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After a while, I decided to be on my own. In a very small way. I never had any people work for me. But I was wholesaling travel goods.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have done that since then.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my own. And now I'm retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] Is it possible my wife should talk about it? You want my part of the story. Very clever girls. The fact that one is-- my elder daughter got into medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your elder daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samira.

speaker: interviewer
text: Samira.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zmira in Hebrew. Zmira. There was a reason for it. It's a combination. In Hebrew, my mother was called Zahava, called her Zahava. And I had a sister called Miriam. So I didn't know at that time I would have two daughters. So I decided to give a combination, Zmira, a bit of this, and a bit of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is for your mother-- named for both your mother and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother and my sister, sorry, yes. She was always very successful. As a matter of fact, when she got into medicine, she got into a scholarship. At that time-- when was that. [INAUDIBLE] exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CAR HORN] Yes. So she got into medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got in here. I suppose in a minute she'll be here. She got into medicine. Yes, my grandsons are here-- [WAVING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? And your other daughter, what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Debbie. And she liked to call herself Deborah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Deborah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Deborah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what does Deborah do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deborah is a solicitor. She doesn't work now, because she's expecting another child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have got three grandchildren. [INAUDIBLE] fourth on the way, I hope. Three boys, and one coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, after all the incredible difficulties of your early life, Australia has been--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know why the Pope, when he comes here, he kisses the earth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know why he kisses the earth in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I would do the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this has been a wonderful haven for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quiet, pleasant, tolerant. The climate is good. The people are good. I think people who are born here do not realize what they have got. A person like me, coming, you know, Europe, Asia, coming to Australia, knows to compare. And they come to the right conclusion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this has been--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the story of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Shmuel, I want to thank you very, very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shmuel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you do the introductions, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should do the introduction? OK, my family. I will start with the basis. My [NON-ENGLISH]. Without her, nothing would be. The result-- my daughter here. My daughter there, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? Who? You haven't introduced us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, Samira, I just told you. I think I would like to repeat what her name-- how come her name Zmira. It's a combination of mother and sister-- two Hebrew names. It's unusual here. I don't think, in Australia, we'll find the word Zmira. But this is this is the secret-- my secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: What should I say? I was glad-- this came about because there's so many things that I didn't even think before it came now while we were talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: As an example, the name of my [INAUDIBLE], she was, as I told I didn't remember for so many years, came up last night. It was a miracle. And for future people, Jewish people, civilized people, my people, my relatives, should know what we passed through. Take a lesson from it. Draw the right consequences. That's all I want to say.

words.text: That's all I want to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Shmuel, you-- you are here when chance really decreed, and the times you lived in decreed you never should have been here. And you've got your wonderful family with you. So, ultimately, you have come through everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce us to the boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson, number one. Not in important, just in chronological, mind you. I mean chronological. Adam? What should I say, Adam? I love him. I adore him. He deserves to be loved. Not less, not less [INAUDIBLE], Jonathan. Everything. I am too poor in words to describe these two grandsons I have got. I will just tell you I am very lucky, very happy with having such grandsons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Rachel, is there something you would like to add to this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid if I'll say something, I'll cry. And then I'll make a fool out of myself. When we started our married life 46 years ago, all we wanted is build on the ashes of all that-- a new life, and build a family, [SOBS] and give them all the love, and all the-- oh, it's make a fool of myself. And I'm very happy that, at this moment, when one makes a summation of the last 50 years, I'm very happy that we succeeded. My husband did not become a bitter man. It didn't overcome him. He was the same kind, nice man he was probably he was born. He tried to go in the steps-- in the way that his father showed him, was probably his mother, too, his family. He tries to instill in his grandchildren this most holy thing for us, the continuation. And that is the lesson of this war. I'm sorry. [SHMUEL BRUSHES TEARS FROM RACHEL'S FACE] I'm sorry. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Samira?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a great dad. The best.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Adam?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to say that we often-- I often talk about the Holocaust, but only in sketches. And I think that once I see the tape in full, I'll really get the whole story out of him, and I'm sure it'll be a very interesting and courageous one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jonathan?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my grandfather showed a lot of bravery and courage. And he'll always have a special place in our hearts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you all, very, very much-- and Shmuel, you in particular.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
